title: Lentils
prose: In South Africa, lentils ( Lens culinaris ) are produced mainly under irrigation in the Lower Orange River area. Local production is confined to the small seed type, with a further classification of two groups namely, lentils with orange coloured seed lobes (black lentils) and lentils with yellow-green seed lobes (grey lentils). The local black and grey lentils can be considered as indigenous types, being the result of natural selection along the Lower Orange River. Production of grey lentils was suspended due to the considerably higher yields obtained with black lentils and also because of numerous problems caused by mixing or blending during harvesting. CLIMATE Thanks to specifically adapted cultivars, lentils are universally grown under a wide range of climatic conditions. Along the Lower Orange River, it is grown as a cool season crop. Although the optimum soil temperature for germination is between 18 and 20 Â°C, satisfactory germination also occurs at lower temperatures. Young plants are fairly resistant to frost and cold, but during the flowering period they are sensitive to frost. Temperatures above 27 Â°C are detrimental to crop development. SOIL Best results are obtained on fertile, well-drained soils. Lentils are much more sensitive to waterlogged soils than to moisture stress. Heavy clay soils and soils with a high water table should therefore be avoided. As lentils are also very sensitive to saline conditions, brackish soils should be avoided. Lentils remove 50 kg N, 6.2 kg P and 32.0 kg K per 1 000 kg of seed produced. FERTILIZATION Nitrogen (N) Being leguminous, the lentil plant provides up to 85% of its own nitrogen requirement through N-fixation. It is therefore important to inoculate the seed with the correct Rhizobium bacteria. In South Africa the inoculant used for peas is also used for lentils. A small quantity of nitrogen (10 to 15 kg N ha -1 ) is recommended at planting as this stimulates seedling development and nitrogen fixation. Additional nitrogen may be required if the nodule bacteria are found wanting. Experience in the Lower Orange River area indicates that this is seldom necessary and that injudicious nitrogen applications often cause problems. Phosphorus (P) Root development in lentils is stimulated by sufficient phosphorus. Research data on fertilization is lacking, hence no detailed P-norms exist. A reliable soil analysis will, however, indicate a deficiency or excess of P in the soil. As a general guideline, 20 kg P ha -1 can be applied. This can be increased if the soil-P status is very low. However, lentils are very sensitive to zinc deficiency, which can be induced by applying too much P. Where lentils are grown in young vineyards along the Lower Orange River, only the vineyards are fertilized, with no additional applications for the lentils. Potassium (K) Lentils have a relatively high uptake of potassium from the soil but seldom react to K-fertilization. K-fertilization is, however, recommended when analyses indicate a low soil-K status. Potassium plays an important role in disease resistance and quality (improves cooking suitability). Micro-elements Lentils are very sensitive to zinc deficiency. On soils with low Zn-levels, or where it is known that a Zn-deficiency exists, zinc should be applied at a rate of 15 to 20 kg zinc fertilizer per hectare, or a compound containing zinc may be used. To date, no other trace element deficiencies in lentils have been positively identified in South Africa.
